mappings.rationaleAI: Museums as institutions dedicated to acquiring, preserving, researching, and displaying culturally significant objects directly address the role of museums and galleries in preservation and dissemination (G.1).
Art's contested definitions and the philosophy of aesthetics examining creativity and interpretation address the aesthetic experience (A) and criticism of works of art (B).
The arts covering aesthetic theory, art criticism, and art education address the aesthetic experience (A), the interaction of arts with social institutions (D), and the arts in education (D.5).
Oral tradition as the transmission of art and culture through speech and performance across generations directly addresses the role of oral tradition in preservation and dissemination (G.5).
Folklore as community-based expressive culture addresses influences of social and cultural conditioning on art apprehension (A.1), the role of oral tradition (G.5), and imitative tradition (G.6).
Musicology as academic research-based study of music encompassing theory, aesthetics, and cultural analysis addresses criticism of works of art (B) and scholarship in the arts (C).
Religion's intersection with sacred texts, institutions, and cultural practices connects to the arts and religion (D.3).
Myth as a genre encoding cosmogonic and theogonic explanations through narrative directly addresses symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b).
Mass media as broadcast, print, and digital channels distributing content addresses the role of mechanical and electronic media in dissemination (G.4).
Television as a telecommunication medium transmitting moving images addresses the role of mechanical and electronic media in dissemination (G.4).
Film as a visual art form connects to special problems of appreciation in theatrical arts (A.4.b) and the role of mechanical and electronic media (G.4).
Painting spanning representational to abstract modes with genre and medium distinctions addresses appreciation in the visual arts (A.4.d) and diverse criteria of evaluation (B).

Literature's academic study through literary criticism and theory addresses appreciation in literature (A.4.a), while copyright and censorship connect to remuneration and protection of artists' rights (E.4).
Music as a cultural universal with academic study fields addresses special problems of appreciation in music (A.4.c).
Visual arts including painting, sculpture, and photography address special problems of appreciation in the visual arts (A.4.d).
Popular culture as commercial art forms address social uses of art (D.1) and the art market as an economic system (E.3).
Culture as social behaviors, norms, and artistic customs addresses influences of social conditioning on art apprehension (A.1) and the interaction of arts with cultural institutions (D).
Freud's psychoanalytic interpretation of art, symbol, and myth addresses meaning in art (A.2.a) and symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b).
Tolstoy's aesthetic philosophy and social-realist literary tradition address the aesthetic experience (A) and social uses of art (D.1).
Van Gogh's paradox of commercial failure in life and posthumous iconic status addresses factors affecting the economic value of art (E.1) and art as a vocation (F.2).
Education's transmission of knowledge and skills connects to the arts in education and aesthetic education (D.5) and the preparation of the artist through training methods (F.1).
Animation as a filmmaking technique using digital technology connects to technology, science, and the arts (D.4) and mechanical and electronic media (G.4).
Performing arts as live audience arts preserved through recording technology connect to preservation of works of art by performance (G.1).
Chaplin's political satire of fascism connects to social control of art (D.2), while his total artistic control exemplifies art as a vocation (F.2).
Property including intellectual property rights connects to remuneration of artists and protection of their rights (E.4).

Pottery as one of humanity's oldest technologies with applications spanning tableware and archaeological artifacts connects to the role of industry and commerce in preservation and dissemination (G.3).

The Islamic Golden Age's intensive cultural production in literature, art, music, and architecture addresses influences of social and cultural conditioning on artistic creation and apprehension (A.1).
The history of music as an academic field addresses resources and methods of scholarship in the arts (C.1) and the relation of scholarship to other disciplines (C.2).
Realism's truthful representation engaging social and political content addresses diverse criteria of evaluation including social and moral significance (B.1).
Akbar's patronage merging Timurid, Perso-Islamic, and Indian artistic traditions addresses social uses of art (D.1) and systems of financing artistic activities through imperial patronage (E.2).
Philosophy's branch of aesthetics examining rational inquiry into artistic value addresses the aesthetic experience (A) and the criticism of works of art (B).
